                "description": "SMWTBW\n\nThe National Weather Service in Ruskin has issued a\n\n* Special Marine Warning for...\nCoastal waters from Tarpon Springs to Suwannee River FL out 20\nNM...\nWaters from Tarpon Springs to Suwannee River FL out 20 to 60 NM...\n\n* Until 600 PM EST.\n\n* At 423 PM EST, severe thunderstorms capable of producing\nwaterspouts were located along a line extending from 6 nm northwest\nof Yankeetown to 61 nm west of Anclote Key, moving east at 25\nknots.\n\nHAZARD...Waterspouts and wind gusts 34 knots or greater.\n\nSOURCE...Radar.\n\nIMPACT...Waterspouts can quickly form and capsize boats, damage\nvessels, and create suddenly higher waves.                 "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow.                 "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Portland OR",
                "headline": "Beach Hazards Statement issued February 15 at 12:44PM PST until February 15 at 10:00PM PST by NWS Portland OR",
                "description": "* WHAT...For both Beach Hazards Statements, sneaker waves\npossible.\n\n* WHERE...North and Central Coast of Oregon, and South\nWashington Coast.\n\n* WHEN...For the first Beach Hazards Statement, until 10 PM PST\nthis evening. For the second Beach Hazards Statement, from\nlate Monday night through Tuesday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Waves can run up significantly farther on a beach than\nnormal, including over rocks and jetties. Sneaker waves can\nsuddenly knock people off of their feet and quickly pull them\ninto the frigid ocean which may lead to serious injury or\ndrowning.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Caution should be used when in or near\nthe water, especially those who will be razor clamming. Keep\nchildren and pets away from the surf zone. Keep off of\njetties, rocks and logs near the surf zone. If you see someone\nswept into the sea do not swim in after them. Call 911 and\nkeep an eye on them until help arrives.",
                "instruction": "Remain out of the water to avoid hazardous swimming conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "event": "Beach Hazards Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Portland OR",
                "headline": "Beach Hazards Statement issued February 15 at 12:44PM PST until February 17 at 10:00PM PST by NWS Portland OR",
                "description": "* WHAT...For both Beach Hazards Statements, sneaker waves\npossible.\n\n* WHERE...North and Central Coast of Oregon, and South\nWashington Coast.\n\n* WHEN...For the first Beach Hazards Statement, until 10 PM PST\nthis evening. For the second Beach Hazards Statement, from\nlate Monday night through Tuesday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Waves can run up significantly farther on a beach than\nnormal, including over rocks and jetties. Sneaker waves can\nsuddenly knock people off of their feet and quickly pull them\ninto the frigid ocean which may lead to serious injury or\ndrowning.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Caution should be used when in or near\nthe water, especially those who will be razor clamming. Keep\nchildren and pets away from the surf zone. Keep off of\njetties, rocks and logs near the surf zone. If you see someone\nswept into the sea do not swim in after them. Call 911 and\nkeep an eye on them until help arrives.",
                "instruction": "Remain out of the water to avoid hazardous swimming conditions.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "CFWPQR"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [

                "headline": "Flood Warning issued February 15 at 2:39PM CST until February 16 at 1:00AM CST by NWS Topeka KS",
                "description": "...The Flood Warning continues for the following rivers in Kansas...\n\nNeosho River Near Neosho Rapids affecting Lyon County.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is occurring and moderate flooding is\nforecast.\n\n* WHERE...Neosho River near Neosho Rapids.\n\n* WHEN...Until just after midnight tonight.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 22.0 feet, Low lying areas along the river flood.\nAt 23.0 feet, Water overtops Road 145 on the west side of the\nbridge over the Neosho River.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 1:30 PM CST Sunday the stage was 22.5 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise to a crest of 23.1\nfeet this evening.                 "headline": "Fire Weather Watch issued February 15 at 1:36PM MST until February 17 at 8:00PM MST by NWS Goodland KS",
                "description": "Southerly winds gusting as high as 40 mph will continue across\nthe warning area Today, before decreasing near sunset.\nFor Tuesday, low relative humidity and sustained winds around 40\nmph could lead to extreme fire spread for any fires that ignite.\nSouthwest winds could change to west behind a cold front in the\nafternoon.\n\n* AFFECTED AREA...Fire Weather Zones 003 Decatur, 004 Norton,\n014 Thomas, 015 Sheridan, 016 Graham, 027 Wallace, 028 Logan,\n029 Gove, 041 Greeley, 042 Wichita and 254 Cheyenne.\n\n* TIMING...From Tuesday morning through Tuesday evening.\n\n* WINDS...West 30 to 40 mph with gusts up to 60 mph.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...As low as 14 percent.\n\n* IMPACTS...Unpredictable fire behavior. Any fires that develop\nmay rapidly grow and spread out of control.",
                "instruction": "A Fire Weather Watch means that critical fire weather conditions\nare forecast to occur. Listen for later forecasts and possible\nred flag warnings.",

                "headline": "Flood Warning issued February 15 at 2:35PM CST until February 16 at 1:30AM CST by NWS Topeka KS",
                "description": "...The Flood Warning continues for the following rivers in Kansas...\n\nCottonwood River near Emporia affecting Lyon County.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is occurring and minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Cottonwood River near Emporia.\n\n* WHEN...Until just after midnight tonight.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 20.0 feet, South bank of the Cottonwood River 1 mile\nsouth of Emporia floods.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 1:30 PM CST Sunday the stage was 21.3 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise to a crest of 21.4\nfeet this evening. It will then fall below flood stage just\nafter midnight tonight.\n- Flood stage is 20.0 feet.",
                "instruction": "Motorists should not attempt to drive around barricades or drive\ncars through flooded areas.\n\nThis product along with additional weather and stream information is\navailable at www.weather.gov/top/.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Duluth MN",
                "headline": "Winter Storm Watch issued February 15 at 2:32PM CST until February 19 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Duluth MN",
                "description": "* WHAT...Blizzard conditions possible. There is an 80 to 95 percent\nchance for total snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches and a 50\nto 90 percent chance for total snow accumulations in excess of 12\ninches. Ice accumulations around a light glaze possible. Winds\ncould gust as high as 50 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Southern Cook and Southern Lake Counties. This includes\nthe Tribal Lands of the Grand Portage Reservation.\n\n* WHEN...From Tuesday evening through Thursday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult to impossible. Areas of\nblowing snow could significantly reduce visibility. The hazardous\nconditions could impact the Tuesday evening, Wednesday, and\nThursday morning commutes.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Expect terrain and lake enhancement to\nsnowfall totals and snowfall rates. The heaviest snowfall rates\nwill be Tuesday night and Wednesday morning. Winds will be\nstrongest Tuesday evening into Wednesday morning. The best\npotential for a wintry mix will be along and southwest of Silver\nBay Tuesday evening into Wednesday morning.",
                "instruction": "Prepare for possible blizzard conditions, especially whiteout\nconditions along Highway 61 on the North Shore. Continue to monitor\nthe latest forecasts for updates on this situation.\n\nPersons should consider delaying travel. If travel is absolutely\nnecessary, drive with extreme caution. Consider taking a winter\nstorm kit along with you, including such items as tire chains,\nbooster cables, flashlight, shovel, blankets and extra clothing.\nAlso take water, a first aid kit, and anything else that would help\nyou survive in case you become stranded.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                "headline": "Winter Storm Watch issued February 15 at 2:32PM CST until February 19 at 12:00PM CST by NWS Duluth MN",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow, patchy blowing snow, and mixed precipitation\npossible. There is a 50 to 90 percent chance for total snow\naccumulations in excess of 6 inches, highest for the higher\nterrain of the North Shore. There is also a 20 to 70 percent\nchance for at least a glaze of ice accumulations. Winds could gust\nas high as 35 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Northern Aitkin, Koochiching, North Cass, Itasca, Carlton,\nSt. Louis, and Northern Cook and Lake Counties. This includes the\nTribal Lands of the Fond du Lac Band, the Mille Lacs Band, Big\nSandy Lake area and the Bois Forte Band, Nett Lake, Deer Creek\nand, Lake Vermilion areas. Other locations including Voyageurs\nNational Park. The entire Boundary Waters is also included.\n\n* WHEN...From Tuesday evening through Thursday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult. The hazardous conditions\ncould impact the Tuesday evening, Wednesday, and Thursday morning\ncommutes.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Periods of gusty winds and visibility of less\nthan one quarter mile are possible in the Winter Storm Watch area.\nThe best potential for a wintry mix will be along and south of the\nIron Range Tuesday evening into Wednesday morning.",

                "headline": "High Wind Watch issued February 15 at 1:21PM MST until February 17 at 5:00PM MST by NWS Goodland KS",
                "description": "* WHAT...West winds 30 to 45 mph with gusts up to 65 mph possible.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of east central and northeast Colorado and\nnorthwest and west central Kansas.\n\n* WHEN...From Tuesday morning through Tuesday afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...High winds may move loose debris, damage property and\ncause power outages. Travel could be difficult, especially for\nhigh profile vehicles. Plumes of blowing dust will create pockets\nof low visibility. Visibilities will likely change rapidly over\nshort distances, making travel hazardous in these areas.",
                "instruction": "Fasten loose objects or shelter objects in a safe location prior to\nthe onset of winds.\n\nWinds this strong can make driving difficult, especially for high\nprofile vehicles. Use extra caution.\n\nBlowing dust can significantly lower air quality and it is\nencouraged that you take preventative measures such as staying\nindoors or wearing protective breathing masks if outside. High dust\nconcentrations can cause respiratory problems, decrease lung\nactivity, aggravate asthma, and lead to potential heart-related\nproblems, especially with children, elderly or those with\npre-existing respiratory conditions.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Anchorage AK",
                "headline": "Blizzard Warning issued February 15 at 11:12AM AKST until February 17 at 12:00AM AKST by NWS Anchorage AK",
                "description": "* WHAT...Blizzard conditions expected. Total snow accumulations\nbetween 3 and 7 inches. Winds gusting as high as 55 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Kuskokwim Delta Coast, north of the Kuskokwim River, and\nWestern Capes.\n\n* WHEN...From 3 AM Monday to midnight AKST Monday Night.\n\n* IMPACTS...Visibilities reduced to one quarter mile or less due to\nblowing snow. Whiteout conditions are expected and will make\ntravel very difficult. Gusty winds could blow around unsecured\nobjects.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A strong storm will move into the Kuskokwim\nRegion tonight, bringing moderate to heavy snowfall, paired with\ngusty SE winds for periods of whiteout conditions; this storm will\nbegin to weaken by Monday night.",
                "instruction": "A Blizzard Warning means severe winter weather conditions are\nexpected or occurring. Falling and blowing snow with strong winds\nand poor visibilities are likely. This will lead to whiteout\nconditions, making travel very hazardous. If you must travel, have a\nwinter survival kit with you. If you get stranded, stay with your\nvehicle.\n\nPersons should consider delaying all travel. If travel is absolutely\nnecessary, drive with extreme caution. Consider taking a winter\nstorm kit along with you, including such items as tire chains,\nbooster cables, flashlight, shovel, blankets and extra clothing.\nAlso take water, a first aid kit, and anything else that would help\nyou survive in case you become stranded.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {

                    ],

                "event": "Rip Current Statement",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS San Juan PR",
                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued February 15 at 4:12PM AST until February 16 at 6:00AM AST by NWS San Juan PR",
                "description": "* WHAT...Life-threatening rip currents.\n\n* WHERE...Beaches across all north exposed areas in Puerto Rico,\nCulebra, St. Thomas, and St. John.\n\n* WHEN...Through late tonight.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water, where it becomes difficult to\nreturn to safety.",
                "instruction": "There is a high risk of rip currents.\n\nRip currents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as groins, jetties\nand piers. Heed the advice of lifeguards, beach patrol flags and\nsigns.\n\nIf you become caught in a rip current, yell for help. Remain\ncalm, do not exhaust yourself and stay afloat while waiting for\nhelp. If you have to swim out of a rip current, swim parallel to\nshore and back toward the beach when possible. Do not attempt to\nswim directly against a rip current as you will tire quickly.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "CFWSJU"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WHCA42 TJSJ 152012"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "HIGH RIP CURRENT RISK REMAINS IN EFFECT THROUGH LATE TONIGHT"
                    ],

                        "EAS",
                        "NWEM",
                        "CMAS"
                    ],
                    "VTEC": [

                    ],

                "headline": "High Surf Advisory issued February 15 at 12:00PM PST until February 19 at 4:00AM PST by NWS San Diego CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Large breaking waves of 8 to 11 feet, with sets to 13\nfeet. Minor coastal flooding will also be a threat.\n\n* WHERE...San Diego County Coastal Areas and Orange County\nCoastal Areas.\n\n* WHEN...From 10 AM Monday to 4 AM PST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Dangerous swimming and surfing conditions and\nlocalized beach erosion. High rip current risk. Minor tidal\noverflow into parking lots and boardwalks also possible.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Surf will peak Wednesday morning through\nWednesday evening, especially for San Diego County. Rip\ncurrents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as groins,\njetties and piers. Heed the advice of lifeguards, beach patrol,\nflags and signs.",
                "instruction": "Inexperienced swimmers should remain out of the water due to\ndangerous surf conditions.",

                "headline": "Wind Advisory issued February 15 at 9:32AM HST until February 16 at 6:00AM HST by NWS Honolulu HI",
                "description": "* WHAT...East winds 20 to 30 mph with gusts over 50 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Kahoolawe, Kauai, Lanai, Maui, Molokai, Oahu, and the Big\nIsland. Areas most at risk include higher terrain, passes and\nareas immediately south and west of the mountains.\n\n* WHEN...Until 6 AM HST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Winds this strong can tear off shingles, knock down tree\nbranches, blow away tents and awnings and make it difficult to\nsteer, especially for drivers of high profile vehicles.",
                "instruction": "Watch out for falling tree branches when walking or driving. Make\nsure tents and awnings are secure or take them down. Be prepared for\npower outages.\n\nSecure trash cans, lawn furniture, and other loose outdoor objects\nthat can be easily blown around by strong winds.",
                "response": "Execute",

                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow and patchy blowing snow possible. There is a 50\npercent chance for total snow accumulations in excess of 6 inches.\nWinds could gust as high as 35 mph.\n\n* WHERE...Portions of north central and northwest Minnesota and\nnortheast and southeast North Dakota.\n\n* WHEN...From late Tuesday night through Wednesday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult.                 "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued February 15 at 11:29AM PST until February 18 at 10:00PM PST by NWS Sacramento CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow expected. Accumulation amounts range from up to\n1 foot 1500 to 2500 feet, 1 to 2 feet at 2500 to 3500 feet and 4\nto 8 feet at higher elevations. Wind gusts of 45 to 55mph expected.\n\n* WHERE...West Slope Northern Sierra Nevada and Western Plumas\nCounty/Lassen Park including Interstate 80 and Highway 50,\nnorthern Shasta County including portions of Interstate 5, the\nCoastal Range, and foothill regions of the Sierra Nevada.\n\n* WHEN...From 10 PM this evening to 10 PM PST Wednesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Dangerous to near impossible travel conditions with\nchain controls and road closures. Low visibility due to a\ncombination of wind and heavy snow.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Snow levels will be around 4500-5500 feet\ntonight through Monday. 2500 to 3500 feet snow levels by Monday\nnight and 1500 to 2500 feet Tuesday into Wednesday.                 "event": "Red Flag Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Rapid City SD",
                "headline": "Red Flag Warning issued February 15 at 12:27PM MST until February 15 at 5:00PM MST by NWS Rapid City SD",
                "description": "...RED FLAG WARNING THIS AFTERNOON FOR PORTIONS OF NORTHEAST WY\nINTO SOUTH CENTRAL SD...\n\n.Unseasonably warm weather will combine with very dry air\n(relative humidity as low as 14 percent) and gusty westerly winds\n(gusts 25 to 35 mph) to create critical fire weather conditions\nthis afternoon for portions of northeastern Wyoming through south-\ncentral South Dakota.\n\n* AFFECTED AREA...Fire Weather Zones 315 Southern Campbell, 317\nWeston County Plains, 321 Southern Black Hills, 322 Fall River\nCounty Area, 325 Custer County Plains, 326 Pine Ridge Area,\n332 Badlands Area, 333 Bennett County Area, 334 Mellette and\nTodd Counties and 335 Tripp County.\n\n* WINDS...West 12 to 25 mph with gusts from 25 to 35 mph.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...As low as 14 percent.\n\n* IMPACTS...The combination of gusty winds and low relative\nhumidity will produce critical fire weather conditions.",
                "instruction": "A Red Flag Warning means that critical fire weather conditions\nare either occurring now, or will shortly. A combination of\nstrong winds, low relative humidity, and warm temperatures can\ncontribute to extreme fire behavior.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "RFWUNR"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WWUS83 KUNR 151927"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [
                        "RED FLAG WARNING REMAINS IN EFFECT UNTIL 5 PM MST /6 PM CST/ THIS AFTERNOON FOR GUSTY WINDS AND LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY FOR FIRE WEATHER ZONES 315, 317, 321, 322, 325, 326, 332, 333, 334, AND 335"
                    ],
                    "BLOCKCHANNEL": [
                        "EAS",

                        "CMAS"
                    ],

                "senderName": "NWS Medford OR",
                "headline": "Winter Weather Advisory issued February 15 at 11:20AM PST until February 19 at 10:00AM PST by NWS Medford OR",
                "description": "...LOW SNOW LEVELS WITH MULTIPLE ROUNDS OF PRECIPITATION...\n\n.A sharp cold front will push through the region Monday night into\nTuesday morning with a cold air mass to follow through at least\nThursday. Snow levels will quickly lower from around 3000 ft during\nthe day Monday to around 1000-1500 ft late Monday night into the\nearly hours of Tuesday. Multiple waves of precipitation will move\nthrough the region while this cold air mass is in place, bringing\nmultiple opportunities for low elevation snow. This will be a long\nduration event, and after the initial front Monday night,\nprecipitation will become more showery, resulting in periods of\nspotty and quick accumulations. The values reflected  below indicate\ntotals over a 72 hour period from 10pm Monday through 10pm Thursday.\nDaytime heating will result in snow levels rising to around\n2000-2500 ft during the daylight hours, then lower back down during\nthe overnight periods. The best chances for accumulating snow down\nto valley floors will be during the overnight and early morning\nhours.\n\n* WHAT...Snow expected. Total snow accumulations 2 to 6 inches, with\n6 to 10 inches expected across the Modoc, along Highway 97 north\nof Chiloquin and the higher terrain of the Warners. Winds gusting\nas high as 40 mph over high and exposed terrain.\n\n* WHERE...Klamath Basin and Winter Rim in Klamath county and Lake\nCounty, Northeast Siskiyou and Northwest Modoc Counties, and Modoc\nCounty.\nIncluding the locations of...Dorris, Macdoel, Tulelake, Adin,\nAlturas, Canby, Day, Likely, Klamath Falls, Olene, Chemult,\nCrescent, Beatty, Bly, Sprague River, Adel, Fort Rock, Lakeview,\nValley Falls, Paisley.\nThis also includes the following passes or notable locations...\nCedar Pass on state highway 239 at 6323 ft.\nWarner Mountain Summit on state highway 140 at 5850 ft.\n\n* WHEN...From 4 AM Tuesday to 10 AM PST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel may be very difficult to impossible. The\nhazardous conditions could impact the Tuesday morning and evening\ncommutes.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...The heaviest snow is expected Monday night\ninto early Tuesday morning.\n\nVisit weather.gov/mfr/winter for snow probabilities.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Medford OR",
                "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued February 15 at 11:20AM PST until February 19 at 10:00AM PST by NWS Medford OR",
                "description": "...LOW SNOW LEVELS WITH MULTIPLE ROUNDS OF PRECIPITATION...\n\n.A sharp cold front will push through the region Monday night into\nTuesday morning with a cold air mass to follow through at least\nThursday. Snow levels will quickly lower from around 3000 ft during\nthe day Monday to around 1000-1500 ft late Monday night into the\nearly hours of Tuesday. Multiple waves of precipitation will move\nthrough the region while this cold air mass is in place, bringing\nmultiple opportunities for low elevation snow. This will be a long\nduration event, and after the initial front Monday night,\nprecipitation will become more showery, resulting in periods of\nspotty and quick accumulations. The values reflected  below indicate\ntotals over a 72 hour period from 10pm Monday through 10pm Thursday.\nDaytime heating will result in snow levels rising to around\n2000-2500 ft during the daylight hours, then lower back down during\nthe overnight periods. The best chances for accumulating snow down\nto valley floors will be during the overnight and early morning\nhours.\n\n* WHAT...Heavy snow expected above 5000 ft through Monday morning\nwith accumulations of 4 to 8 inches expected. After a brief lull\nin precipitation Monday afternoon, snow levels drop to around\n1000-1500 ft  Monday night into Tuesday, and remain low through\nThursday. Additional snow accumulations 6 to 12 inches are\nexpected with 2 to 3 ft expected above 5000 ft. Winds gusting as\nhigh as 40 mph over high and exposed terrain.\n\n* WHERE...In western Siskiyou County, elevations above 5000 ft\nthrough Monday morning, lowering all areas Monday evening. This\nincludes all of Highway 96 and 3, and Etna Summit on Sawyers Bar\nRd. This also now includes the Klamath River and Scott Valleys,\nthe communities of Happy Camp, Somes Bar, Etna, Greenview, and\nCallahan.\n\n* WHEN...Until 10 AM PST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel may be very difficult to impossible. The\nhazardous conditions could impact the Monday morning and evening\ncommutes.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...The heaviest snow is expected late Monday\nnight into Tuesday morning.\n\nVisit weather.gov/mfr/winter for snow probabilities.",
                "instruction": "If you must travel, keep an extra flashlight, food, and water in\nyour vehicle in case of an emergency. Call 511 or visit\nquickmap.dot.ca.gov for road information.",

                "description": "...LOW SNOW LEVELS WITH MULTIPLE ROUNDS OF PRECIPITATION...\n\n.A sharp cold front will push through the region Monday night into\nTuesday morning with a cold air mass to follow through at least\nThursday. Snow levels will quickly lower from around 3000 ft during\nthe day Monday to around 1000-1500 ft late Monday night into the\nearly hours of Tuesday. Multiple waves of precipitation will move\nthrough the region while this cold air mass is in place, bringing\nmultiple opportunities for low elevation snow. This will be a long\nduration event, and after the initial front Monday night,\nprecipitation will become more showery, resulting in periods of\nspotty and quick accumulations.                 "description": "...LOW SNOW LEVELS WITH MULTIPLE ROUNDS OF PRECIPITATION...\n\n.A sharp cold front will push through the region Monday night into\nTuesday morning with a cold air mass to follow through at least\nThursday.                 "headline": "Fire Weather Watch issued February 15 at 12:14PM MST until February 17 at 7:00PM MST by NWS Albuquerque NM",
                "description": "...EXTENDED PERIOD OF RAPID FIRE SPREAD ACROSS EASTERN NEW MEXICO...\n\n.A storm system approaching from the west will allow south winds to\nincrease on Monday. This combined with relative humidity values in\nthe low teens will result in critical fire weather conditions across\nparts of northeast NM Monday afternoon. Much stronger to damaging\nsouthwest to west winds will impact New Mexico Tuesday with critical\nfire weather over a larger area of eastern NM. Potential for peak\nwind gusts of 55 to 70 mph with minimum humidity values of 15 to 20%\nwill allow for rapid fire spread among fine fuels.\n\nThe National Weather Service in Albuquerque has issued a Red Flag\nWarning...which is in effect from 11 AM to 6 PM MST Monday. A\nFire Weather Watch has also been issued. This Fire Weather Watch\nis in effect from Tuesday morning through Tuesday evening. The\nFire Weather Watch is no longer in effect.\n\n* AREA AND TIMING...Northeast Highlands (Zone 123) Monday\nafternoon.\n\n* 20 FOOT WINDS...For Monday afternoon, southwest 20 to 25 mph\nwith gusts up to 40 mph. For Tuesday afternoon and evening,\nsouthwest to west 30 to 40 mph with peak gusts of 60 to 70 mph.\n\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...For Monday afternoon, minimum values of 10\nto 15%. For Tuesday afternoon, minimum values of 20 to 30%\n\n* IMPACTS...Any fires that develop will likely spread rapidly.\nOutdoor burning is not recommended.",
                "instruction": "Please advise the appropriate officials or fire crews in the\nfield of this Red Flag Warning.\n\nPlease advise the appropriate officials or fire crews in the\nfield of this Fire Weather Watch.",

                "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued February 15 at 11:03AM PST until February 18 at 10:00PM PST by NWS Hanford CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow  with total snow accumulations 3 to 5 feet above\n6,000 feet with 5 to 7 feet over the highest elevations and 1 to 2\nfeet down to 4,000 feet. The\nhazardous conditions could impact the Monday morning and evening\ncommutes. Strong winds could cause tree damage.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Snow levels will begin at 5,500 to 6,000 feet\non Sunday night and lower to 4,000 feet Tuesday morning. Light\nsnow accumulations down to 3,000 feet Tuesday night into Wednesday.",

                "instruction": "Monitor the latest forecasts for updates on this situation.\n\nPersons should delay all travel if possible.                 "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued February 15 at 1:42PM EST until February 16 at 4:00AM EST by NWS Miami FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerous rip currents.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal Palm Beach County, Coastal Broward County and\nCoastal Miami Dade County.\n\n* WHEN...Through late tonight.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water.",
                "instruction": "Swim near a lifeguard. If caught in a rip current, relax and\nfloat. Don't swim against the current. If able, swim in a\ndirection following the shoreline. If unable to escape, face the\nshore and call or wave for help.",

                "headline": "Fire Weather Watch issued February 15 at 12:35PM CST until February 17 at 8:00PM CST by NWS Hastings NE",
                "description": "...CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS LIKELY THIS AFTERNOON AND\nAGAIN TUESDAY AFTERNOON...\n\n...A FIRE WEATHER WATCH WILL BE IN EFFECT FROM TUESDAY AFTERNOON\nTHROUGH TUESDAY EVENING FOR WIND AND LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY FOR\nDAWSON, FURNAS, GOSPER, SHERMAN AND VALLEY COUNTIES...\n\nThe National Weather Service in Hastings has issued a Fire\nWeather Watch for wind and low relative humidity, which is in\neffect from Tuesday afternoon through Tuesday evening.\n\n* WINDS...For today, southwesterly winds between 10 to 15 MPH with\ngusts as high as 25 to 30 MPH. For Tuesday, southwest winds\nbetween 20 to 30 MPH with gusts up to 30 to 45 MPH.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...As low as around 15 to 25 percent for this\nafternoon, and as low as around 10 to 20 percent for Tuesday\nafternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Any fire that develops will spread quickly. Outdoor\nburning is not recommended.",
                "instruction": "A Red Flag Warning means that critical fire weather conditions\nare either occurring now, or will shortly. A combination of\nstrong winds, low relative humidity, and warm temperatures can\ncontribute to extreme fire behavior.\n\nA Fire Weather Watch means that critical fire weather conditions\nare forecast to occur. Listen for later forecasts and possible\nRed Flag Warnings.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Denver CO",
                "headline": "Red Flag Warning issued February 15 at 11:26AM MST until February 16 at 6:00PM MST by NWS Denver CO",
                "description": "...PROLONGED PERIOD OF CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS TODAY\nTHROUGH AT LEAST MID NEXT WEEK...\n\n...EXTREMELY CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS POSSIBLE TUESDAY...\n\n.Recent dry conditions combining with above normal temperatures\nand periods of gusty winds will bring potentially critical fire\nweather conditions today and Monday. Stronger, more widespread\nwesterly winds are expected to develop Tuesday, possibly producing\nextremely critical fire weather conditions with wind gusts as\nhigh as 65 mph across the plains.\n\n...RED FLAG WARNING IN EFFECT FROM 11 AM TO 6PM MST MONDAY FOR\nWIND AND LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY FOR WASHINGTON AND LINCOLN\nCOUNTIES...\n\n...FIRE WEATHER WATCH REMAINS IN EFFECT FROM TUESDAY\nMORNING THROUGH TUESDAY EVENING FOR WIND AND LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY\nFOR WASHINGTON AND LINCOLN COUNTIES...\n\nThe National Weather Service in Denver has issued a Red Flag\nWarning for wind and low relative humidity, which is in effect\nfrom 11 AM to 6 PM MST Monday.                 "headline": "Red Flag Warning issued February 15 at 11:26AM MST until February 15 at 5:00PM MST by NWS Denver CO",
                "description": "...PROLONGED PERIOD OF CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS TODAY\nTHROUGH AT LEAST MID NEXT WEEK...\n\n...EXTREMELY CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS POSSIBLE TUESDAY...\n\n.Recent dry conditions combining with above normal temperatures\nand periods of gusty winds will bring potentially critical fire\nweather conditions today and Monday. Stronger, more widespread\nwesterly winds are expected to develop Tuesday, possibly producing\nextremely critical fire weather conditions with wind gusts as\nhigh as 65 mph across the plains.\n\n...RED FLAG WARNING IN EFFECT FROM 11 AM TO 6PM MST MONDAY FOR\nWIND AND LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY FOR WASHINGTON AND LINCOLN\nCOUNTIES...\n\n...FIRE WEATHER WATCH REMAINS IN EFFECT FROM TUESDAY\nMORNING THROUGH TUESDAY EVENING FOR WIND AND LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY\nFOR WASHINGTON AND LINCOLN COUNTIES...\n\nThe National Weather Service in Denver has issued a Red Flag\nWarning for wind and low relative humidity, which is in effect\nfrom 11 AM to 6 PM MST Monday. For the second Red Flag Warning, from 11 AM to 6 PM\nMST Monday.                 "instruction": "A Red Flag Warning means that critical fire weather conditions\nare either occurring now....or will shortly.                 "headline": "Fire Weather Watch issued February 15 at 11:26AM MST until February 17 at 8:00PM MST by NWS Denver CO",
                "description": "...PROLONGED PERIOD OF CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS TODAY\nTHROUGH AT LEAST MID NEXT WEEK...\n\n...EXTREMELY CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS POSSIBLE TUESDAY...\n\n.Recent dry conditions combining with above normal temperatures\nand periods of gusty winds will bring potentially critical fire\nweather conditions today and Monday. Stronger, more widespread\nwesterly winds are expected to develop Tuesday, possibly producing\nextremely critical fire weather conditions with wind gusts as\nhigh as 65 mph across the plains.\n\n...FIRE WEATHER WATCH REMAINS IN EFFECT FROM TUESDAY MORNING\nTHROUGH TUESDAY EVENING FOR WIND AND LOW RELATIVE HUMIDITY FOR\nTHE DENVER METRO AND MORGAN COUNTY...\n\n* AFFECTED AREA...Fire Weather Zones 240 and 244.\n\n* TIMING...For the first Fire Weather Watch, from Monday morning\nthrough Monday afternoon. For the second Fire Weather Watch,\nfrom Tuesday morning through Tuesday evening.\n\n* WINDS...Southwest winds 10 to 20 mph with gusts to 30 mph on\nMonday. West winds 30 to 40 mph with gusts up to 60 mph on\nTuesday.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...As low as 10 percent.\n\n* IMPACTS...Conditions will be favorable for rapid fire spread.\nAvoid outdoor burning and any activity that may produce a\nspark and start a wildfire.",
                "instruction": "A Fire Weather Watch means that critical fire weather conditions\nare forecast to occur. Listen for later forecasts and possible\nRed Flag Warnings.",

                "senderName": "NWS Fairbanks AK",
                "headline": "Winter Storm Watch issued February 15 at 9:23AM AKST until February 18 at 3:00PM AKST by NWS Fairbanks AK",
                "description": "* WHAT...Blizzard conditions possible. Total snow accumulations\nbetween 6 and 12 inches possible. Winds could gust as high as 60\nmph.\n\n* WHERE...Bering Strait Coast, Eastern Norton Sound and Nulato\nHills, Interior Seward Peninsula, Shishmaref, Southern Seward\nPeninsula Coast, St Lawrence Island, and Yukon Delta Coast.\n\n* WHEN...From Monday afternoon through Wednesday afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult. Areas of blowing snow\ncould significantly reduce visibility.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...A strong front will bring very strong\neast-southeast winds, heavy snow and blizzard conditions across\nmost of the West Coast.                 "description": "* WHAT...Southwest winds 35 to 40 mph with gusts up to 60 mph\npossible in the plains.                 "description": "...FIRE WEATHER WATCH IN EFFECT FROM TUESDAY AFTERNOON THROUGH\nTUESDAY EVENING FOR 20 FOOT WINDS OF 20 MPH OR GREATER, RELATIVE\nHUMIDITY NEAR 15 PERCENT, AND HIGH FIRE DANGER ACROSS SOUTHEAST\nNEW MEXICO INTO GAINES COUNTY, TEXAS...\n\n* WINDS...West 30 to 40 mph with gusts up to 60 mph.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...As low as 15 percent.\n\n* RFTI...4 or near critical.\n\n* IMPACTS...Any fires that develop will likely spread rapidly.\nOutdoor burning is not recommended.\n\n* SEVERITY...\n\nFUELS (ERC)...50th-70th percentile...2 (out of 5).\n\nWEATHER...Near Critical...2 (out of 5).\n\nFIRE ENVIRONMENT...4 (out of 10).",

                "description": "* WHAT...Snow expected. Total snow accumulations of 4 to 8 inches\nfrom Monday morning through Monday night.                 "instruction": "Have a winter emergency driving kit readily available.                 "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued February 15 at 9:57AM PST until February 19 at 9:00AM PST by NWS San Diego CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow expected. For first round Monday into early\nTuesday, 6-10 inches expected between 6000-7000 ft, 10-18 inches\nexpected 7000-8000 ft, and up to 30 inches above 8000 ft. South\nwinds gusting to 40-60 mph on the ridges and desert slopes.\nOccasional light snow showers Tuesday. Widespread moderate snow\nlate Tuesday into Wednesday morning, with additional totals 8-15\ninches between 6000-7500 ft. Light snow showers expected Thursday.\nStronger southwest winds with gusts in excess of 70 mph along the\ndesert slopes possible late Tuesday and Wednesday. A few\nadditional inches of snow expected Thursday.\n\n* WHERE...San Bernardino County Mountains.\n\n* WHEN...From 10 AM Monday to 9 AM PST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult to impossible. The\nhazardous conditions could impact the Monday morning and evening\ncommutes. Very strong winds could cause extensive tree damage.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...For the first round Monday, snow levels will\nstart around 6800-7500 ft and fall to near 5000 ft by early\nTuesday morning. Snow levels lowering to around 4000 ft Wednesday\nmorning. Impacts to I-15 near the summit of the Cajon Pass\npossible Wednesday morning and Thursday morning.",

                "headline": "Red Flag Warning issued February 15 at 10:31AM MST until February 16 at 5:00PM MST by NWS Riverton WY",
                "description": "* IMPACTS: Low Humidities...Unseasonably Warm Temperatures...and\nStrong Gusty Winds could cause erratic fire behavior.\n\n* AFFECTED AREA:\nIn Central WY Fire Zone....280.\nIn North Central WY Fire Zone....281.\n\n* COUNTIES AFFECTED:\nIn Central WY...Natrona.\nIn North Central WY...Johnson.\n\n* WIND: For Sunday, southwest 15 to 25 mph with gusts up to 40 mph.\nWinds decrease Sunday night.                 "instruction": "A Red Flag Warning means that critical fire weather conditions are\neither occurring now....or will shortly. A combination of strong\nwinds...low relative humidity...dry cured fuels and unseasonably\nwarm temperatures can contribute to extreme fire behavior.",

                "senderName": "NWS Tiyan GU",
                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued February 16 at 3:19AM ChST until February 17 at 5:00PM ChST by NWS Tiyan GU",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerous rip currents.\n\n* WHERE...East facing reefs of the Marianas.\n\n* WHEN...Through Tuesday afternoon.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...Elevated trade-wind swell maintains a high\nrisk of rip currents along east facing reefs through Tuesday\nafternoon.",
                "instruction": "Rip currents will be life threatening. If caught in a rip\ncurrent, relax and float. Don't swim against the current. If\nable, swim in a direction following the shoreline. If unable to\nescape, face the shore and call or wave for help.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "instruction": "If travel is absolutely necessary, drive with extreme caution and be\nprepared for sudden changes in visibility.                 "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued February 15 at 9:07AM PST until February 19 at 9:00AM PST by NWS Los Angeles/Oxnard CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow possible. Total snow accumulations of up to 4\ninches between 3500 and 4500 feet, 6 to 12 inches for 4500 to 6000\nfeet, and 1 to 2 feet above 6000 feet. Wind gusts of up to 70 mph\nthrough Monday afternoon, and as high as 50 mph through Thursday\nmorning.\n\n* WHERE...Eastern San Gabriel Mountains and Northern Ventura County\nMountains.\n\n* WHEN...From 6 AM Monday to 9 AM PST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Roads, and especially bridges and overpasses, will\nlikely become slick and hazardous. Travel could become\ntreacherous. Visibilities may drop below one-quarter mile due to\nfalling and blowing snow. Damaging winds could blow down trees and\npower lines.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...There will be a break in the snowfall for\nTuesday into Tuesday night, before heavy snow returns by\nWednesday. However, blowing and drifting snow producing\nsignificant visibility restrictions will continue above 6000 feet\nelevation, even during the break in snowfall.",
                "instruction": "Monitor the latest forecasts for updates on this situation.\n\nIf travel is absolutely necessary, drive with extreme caution and be\nprepared for sudden changes in visibility. Leave plenty of room\nbetween you and the motorist ahead of you, and allow extra time to\nreach your destination. Avoid sudden braking or acceleration, and be\nespecially cautious on hills or when making turns. Make sure your\ncar is winterized and in good working order.\n\nDamaging winds could blow down trees and power lines. Travel could\nbe difficult, especially for high profile vehicles.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                    ],

                "description": "* AFFECTED AREA...Fire weather zones 417, 418, 428, 429, 430,\n431, 432, 433 in Wyoming. Fire weather zones 434, 435, 436,\n437 in western Nebraska.\n\n* WIND...West to southwest winds at 15 to 20 MPH with gusts up to\n35 MPH.\n\n* HUMIDITY...12 to 17 percent.\n\n* IMPACTS...Any fires that develop will likely spread rapidly.\nOutdoor burning is not recommended.",
                "instruction": "A Red Flag Warning means that critical fire weather conditions\nare either occurring now, or will shortly. A combination of\nstrong winds, low relative humidity, and warm temperatures can\ncontribute to extreme fire behavior.",

                "description": "* WHAT...South winds 20 to 30 kt with gusts up to 35 kt and seas\n8 to 10 ft.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal Waters from Pigeon Point to Point Pinos\nCalifornia out to 10 NM.\n\n* WHEN...Until 9 PM PST this evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Strong winds will cause hazardous seas which could\ncapsize or damage vessels and reduce visibility.",

                "event": "Air Quality Alert",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Detroit/Pontiac MI",
                "headline": "Air Quality Alert issued February 15 at 10:55AM EST by NWS Detroit/Pontiac MI",
                "description": "The Michigan Department of Environment, Great Lakes, and Energy has\nissued an Air Quality Advisory for elevated levels of fine\nparticulate (PM2.5) in Southeast Michigan. Pollutants within these areas\nare expected to be in the Unhealthy for Sensitive Groups range.\n\nThe advisory is in effect for the following Michigan counties...\n\nSt. Clair...Livingston...Oakland...Macomb...Washtenaw...Wayne and\nMonroe.\n\nIt is recommended that, when possible, you avoid strenuous outdoor\nactivities, especially those with heart disease and respiratory\ndiseases like asthma. Monitor for symptoms such as wheezing,\ncoughing, chest tightness, dizziness, or burning in nose, throat,\nand eyes. Reduce or eliminate activities that contribute to air\npollution, such as: outdoor burning, use of residential wood\nburning devices.\n\nTips for households: Keep windows closed overnight to prevent\nsmoke from getting indoors and, if possible, run central air\nconditioning with MERV-13 or higher rated filters.\n\nFor up-to-date air quality data for Michigan visit the MiAir site:\nhttps://air-egle.hub.arcgis.com\n\nFor up-to-date air quality data nationally visit EPAs Air Now site:\nhttps://www.airnow.gov/\n\nFor further health information, please see MDHHS Wildfire Smoke\nand Your Health site:\n\nhttps://www.michigan.gov/mdhhs/safety-injury-prev/environmental-health/\nyour-health-and-wildfire-smoke",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Monitor",
                "parameters": {

                    ],

                        "AEUS73 KDTX 151555"
                    ],

                    ],

                    ]

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Reno NV",
                "headline": "Avalanche Watch issued February 15 at 7:55AM PST by NWS Reno NV",
                "description": "AVAREV\n\nThe following message is transmitted at the request of the U.S.\nForest Service Sierra Avalanche Center.\n\nThe Sierra Avalanche Center in Truckee has issued a BACKCOUNTRY\nAVALANCHE WATCH for the following areas: NWS Reno NV - NVZ002\n(Greater Lake Tahoe)...CAZ072 (Greater Lake Tahoe (CA))\n\n* WHAT...HIGH avalanche danger is anticipated in the backcountry\nMonday evening, lasting through Tuesday night, and possibly into\nWednesday.\n\n* WHERE...Central Sierra Nevada Mountains between Yuba Pass (Hwy\n49) on the north and Ebbetts Pass (Hwy 4) on the south,\nincluding the greater Lake Tahoe area. This applies only to\nbackcountry areas, not to ski areas and highways where avalanche\nmitigation programs exist.\n\n* WHEN...In effect from Sun 07:45 PST to Tue 05:00 PST.\n\n* IMPACTS...A powerful storm with rapidly accumulating snowfall\nand strong winds may cause widespread avalanche activity in the\nmountains. Large avalanches capable of burying or injuring\npeople could occur in backcountry areas.\n\n* PRECAUTIONARY / PREPAREDNESS ACTIONS...Very dangerous avalanche\nconditions could occur in the backcountry. Traveling in, near,\nor below backcountry avalanche terrain during HIGH avalanche\ndanger is not recommended.\n\nConsult https://www.sierraavalanchecenter.org/ or\nwww.avalanche.org for more detailed information.\n\nSimilar avalanche danger may exist at locations outside the\ncoverage area of this or any avalanche center.",
                "instruction": null,

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Melbourne FL",
                "headline": "Rip Current Statement issued February 15 at 10:45AM EST until February 16 at 4:00AM EST by NWS Melbourne FL",
                "description": "* WHAT...Dangerous rip currents and rough surf, and a northward-\nflowing longshore current.\n\n* WHERE...Coastal Volusia, Brevard, Indian River, Saint Lucie, and\nMartin Counties.\n\n* WHEN...Through late tonight.\n\n* IMPACTS...Rip currents can sweep even the best swimmers away\nfrom shore into deeper water.",
                "instruction": "Rip currents are powerful channels of water flowing quickly away\nfrom shore, which occur most often at low spots or breaks in the\nsandbar and in the vicinity of structures such as jetties and\npiers. Heed the advice of lifeguards...beach patrol flags and\nsigns.\n\nEntering the surf is strongly discouraged. If caught in a rip\ncurrent, relax and float. Don't swim against the current. If\nable, swim in a direction following the shoreline. If unable to\nescape, face the shore and call or wave for help.",
                "response": "Avoid",

                "description": "AVAVEF\n\nThe Eastern Sierra Avalanche Center in Mammoth Lakes\nhas issued a Backcountry Avalanche Watch for the\nfollowing areas: the Eastern Sierra in Mono County and\nthe Eastern Sierra in Inyo County\n\n* WHAT...The avalanche danger is expected to rise to HIGH by\ntomorrow afternoon and remain elevated into mid-week.\n\n* WHERE...The eastern slopes of the Sierra Nevada mountain range\nbetween Virgina Lakes on the north to Bishop Creek on the\nsouth.\n\n* WHEN...In effect from Sun 07:00 PST to Mon 07:00 PST.\n\n* IMPACTS...|Heavy snow and extreme winds will create widespread\nareas of unstable snow.",

                "event": "Avalanche Watch",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Reno NV",
                "headline": "Avalanche Watch issued February 15 at 7:10AM PST by NWS Reno NV",
                "description": "AVAREV\n\nThe following message is transmitted at the request of the\nEastern Sierra Avalanche Center.\n\nThe Eastern Sierra Avalanche Center in Mammoth Lakes has issued a\nBACKCOUNTRY AVALANCHE WATCH for the following areas:\n\nNWS Las Vegas NV - CA-519 (Eastern Sierra in Inyo County)\nNWS Reno NV - CA-073 (Eastern Sierra in Mono County)\n\n* WHAT...The avalanche danger is expected to rise to HIGH by\nMonday afternoon (February 16) and remain elevated through mid-\nweek.\n\n* WHERE...The eastern Sierra Nevada from Virginia Lakes to the\nsouthern end of Bishop Creek.\n\n* WHEN...In effect from Monday 7 AM PST to Tuesday 7 AM PST.\n\n* IMPACTS...Heavy snow and extreme winds will create widespread\nareas of unstable snow.\n\n* PRECAUTIONARY / PREPAREDNESS ACTIONS...Very dangerous avalanche\nconditions are expected to develop. Avalanches may run long\ndistances, into mature forests, valley floors, or flat terrain.\nTravel in avalanche terrain is not recommended.\n\nConsult http://www.esavalanche.org/ or www.avalanche.org for more\ndetailed information.\n\nSimilar avalanche danger may exist at locations outside the\ncoverage area of this or any avalanche center.",
                "instruction": null,
                "response": "Monitor",

                "senderName": "NWS Memphis TN",
                "headline": "Flood Warning issued February 15 at 8:39AM CST by NWS Memphis TN",
                "description": "...The Flood Warning is extended for the following rivers in\nTennessee...\n\nSouth Fork of the Forked Deer near Halls\n\nFor the South Fork Forked Deer River...including Jackson, Halls...\nMinor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is occurring and minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...South Fork of the Forked Deer near Halls.\n\n* WHEN...From this morning until further notice.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 11.5 feet, Farmland near the river is flooded.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 8:00 AM CST Sunday the stage was 9.2 feet.\n- Recent Activity...The maximum river stage in the 24 hours\nending at 8:00 AM CST Sunday was 9.2 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise to a crest of 11.5\nfeet this afternoon.\n- Flood stage is 9.0 feet.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Turn around, don't drown when encountering flooded roads. Most flood\ndeaths occur in vehicles.\n\nAdditional information is available at weather.gov/memphis.\n\nThe next statement will be issued as needed.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "FLSMEG"
                    ],

                "headline": "Flood Warning issued February 15 at 8:33AM CST by NWS Little Rock AR",
                "description": "...The National Weather Service in Little Rock AR has issued a Flood\nWarning for the following rivers in Arkansas...\n\nCache River Near Patterson affecting Jackson and Woodruff\nCounties.\n\nFor the Cache River...including Patterson...Minor flooding is\nforecast.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Cache River near Patterson.\n\n* WHEN...From Monday morning until further notice.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 9.0 feet, Low swampy timberland along the river\nbegins to flood. Flood gates should be closed and equipment moved\nout of the low grounds along the river and tributaries.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 8:15 AM CST Sunday the stage was 7.3 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise above flood stage\nMonday morning and continue rising to a crest of 9.5 feet\nTuesday evening.\n- Flood stage is 9.0 feet.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Turn around, don't drown when encountering flooded roads. Most flood\ndeaths occur in vehicles.\n\nRiver forecasts are based on current conditions and rainfall\nforecasted to occur over the next 24 hours. During periods of\nflooding...Evening forecasts are reissued with updated rainfall\nforecasts.\n\nObserved and forecasted stage data plots are available on our\nAdvanced Hydrologic Prediction Service web page at...\nwww.weather.gov/lzk\n\nUnder the Current Conditions section...Select River and Lakes AHPS.\n\nThe next statement will be issued Monday morning at 845 AM CST.",

                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS San Francisco CA",
                "headline": "Coastal Flood Advisory issued February 15 at 6:24AM PST until February 17 at 3:00PM PST by NWS San Francisco CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Up to 1.5 ft of inundation above ground level is\npossible in low-lying areas near shorelines and tidal\nwaterways.\n\n* WHERE...Monterey Bay.\n\n* WHEN...From 4 AM Sunday to 3 PM PST Tuesday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Flooding of lots, parks, and roads with only\nisolated road closures expected.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...At the Monterey tidal gauge, high\ntide is expected to be:\n1.0 ft above normal (6.3 ft MLLW) at 8:28 AM Sunday\n1.5 ft above normal (6.8 ft MLLW) at 9:07 AM Monday\n1.2 ft above normal (6.6 ft MLLW) at 9:46 AM Tuesday\nThese prediction include up to 1.0 ft of storm surge that will\nenhance the astronomical tide and flooding threat.",
                "instruction": "If travel is required, allow extra time as some roads may be\nclosed. Do not drive around barricades or through water of\nunknown depth. Take the necessary actions to protect flood-prone\nproperty.",
                "response": "Monitor",

                "senderName": "NWS Topeka KS",
                "headline": "Flood Warning issued February 15 at 7:51AM CST until February 16 at 9:00AM CST by NWS Topeka KS",
                "description": "* WHAT...Flooding caused by the Marais Des Cygnes River rising above\nflood stage of 20 feet.\n\n* WHERE...A portion of east central Kansas, including the following\ncounty, Franklin.\n\n* WHEN...Until 900 AM CST Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Flooding of the Marais Des Cygnes River, northeast of\nPomona will continue through the mid morning hours of Monday.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 747 AM CST, Flooding is occurring along the Marais Des\nCygnes River bank northeast of Pomona.\n- At 730 AM CST, the Marais Des Cygnes River, northeast of\nPomona was at 22.39 feet and may crest around 24 feet this\nafternoon.\n- Some locations that will experience flooding include...\nmainly rural areas of Western Franklin County along the\nMarais Des Cygnes river northeast of Pomona.",
                "instruction": "Turn around, don't drown when encountering flooded roads. Most flood\ndeaths occur in vehicles.",
                "response": "Avoid",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "FLWTOP"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [

                "event": "Red Flag Warning",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS North Platte NE",
                "headline": "Red Flag Warning issued February 15 at 7:01AM CST until February 15 at 6:00PM CST by NWS North Platte NE",
                "description": "...CRITICAL FIRE WEATHER CONDITIONS EXPECTED THIS AFTERNOON...\n\n.A combination of near record high temperatures, low relative\nhumidity, and gusty southwest winds are expected to lead to\ncritical fire weather conditions across all of western and north\ncentral Nebraska.\n\n* AFFECTED AREA...Fire Weather Zone 204 Eastern\nPanhandle/Crescent Lake NWR, Fire Weather Zone 206\nSandhills/Valentine NWR/Nebraska National Forest, Fire Weather\nZone 208 Niobrara Valley/Fort Niobrara NWR/Samuel R McKelvie\nNational Forest, Fire Weather Zone 209 Loup Rivers Basin, Fire\nWeather Zone 210 Frenchman Basin and Fire Weather Zone 219\nLoess Plains.\n\n* TIMING...Noon CST through 6 PM CST or 11 AM MST through 5 PM\nMST.\n\n* WINDS...Southwest 10 to 20 mph with gusts up to 30 mph.\n\n* RELATIVE HUMIDITY...As low as 13 percent.\n\n* TEMPERATURES...Up to 70.\n\n* LIGHTNING...Not expected\n\n* IMPACTS...Any fire starts will rapidly grow and spread.",
                "instruction": "A Red Flag Warning means that critical fire weather conditions\nare either occurring now, or will shortly. A combination of\nstrong winds, low relative humidity, and warm temperatures can\ncontribute to extreme fire behavior.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {
                    "AWIPSidentifier": [
                        "RFWLBF"
                    ],
                    "WMOidentifier": [
                        "WWUS83 KLBF 151301"
                    ],
                    "NWSheadline": [

                    ],

                "description": "* WHAT...For the first High Surf Advisory, large breaking waves\nof 8 to 12 feet with dangerous rip currents. For the second\nHigh Surf Advisory, large breaking waves of 10 to 15 feet with\nlocal sets to 20 feet on Wednesday and dangerous rip currents.\n\n* WHERE...San Luis Obispo County Beaches, Santa Barbara County\nCentral Coast Beaches, Malibu Coast and Los Angeles County\nBeaches.\n\n* WHEN...From 10 AM Monday to 6 PM PST Thursday.\n\n* IMPACTS...There is an increased risk for ocean drowning. Rip\ncurrents can pull swimmers and surfers out to sea.                 "instruction": "Remain out of the water due to dangerous surf conditions, or stay\nnear occupied lifeguard towers. Rock jetties can be deadly in\nsuch conditions, stay off the rocks.",

                "description": "* WHAT...For the High Rip Current Risk, dangerous rip currents\nexpected. For the High Surf\nAdvisory, from 11 AM EST /10 AM CST/ this morning to 5 AM EST\n/4 AM CST/ Monday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Dangerous swimming and surfing conditions and\nlocalized beach erosion. Rip currents can sweep even the best\nswimmers away from shore into deeper water.",
                "instruction": "Inexperienced swimmers should remain out of the water due to\ndangerous surf conditions.\n\nSwim near a lifeguard. If caught in a rip current, relax and\nfloat.                 "senderName": "NWS Shreveport LA",
                "headline": "Flood Warning issued February 14 at 10:43PM CST until February 17 at 6:00AM CST by NWS Shreveport LA",
                "description": "...The National Weather Service in Shreveport has issued a Flood\nWarning for the following rivers in Texas...\n\nNeches River Near Neches affecting Cherokee and Anderson Counties.\n\nFor the Neches River...including Neches, Alto, Diboll, Rockland...\nMinor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHAT...Minor flooding is forecast.\n\n* WHERE...Neches River near Neches.\n\n* WHEN...From late Sunday night to Tuesday morning.\n\n* IMPACTS...At 12.0 feet, Expect minor lowland flooding of the\nheavily wooded floodplain. Ranchers that may have livestock and\nequipment in the river bottoms should move them to higher ground.\nExpect minor flooding of the boat ramp.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- At 9:30 PM CST Saturday the stage was 6.1 feet.\n- Forecast...The river is expected to rise above flood stage early\nMonday morning to a crest of 12.1 feet Monday morning. It will\nthen fall below flood stage early Monday afternoon.\n- Flood stage is 12.0 feet.\n- Flood History...This crest compares to a previous crest of 12.4\nfeet on 06/24/2015.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "Do not drive cars through flooded areas.                 "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Fairbanks AK",
                "headline": "Winter Storm Warning issued February 14 at 12:37PM AKST until February 15 at 9:00PM AKST by NWS Fairbanks AK",
                "description": "* WHAT...Heavy snow expected. Additional snow accumulations between\n4 to 8 inches. Winds gusting as high as 40 mph over high terrain\nand 30 mph in valleys, strongest Saturday night and early Sunday\nmorning.\n\n* WHERE...Central Interior, Fairbanks North Star Borough, Nenana\nHills, Nenana, Northern Denali Borough, White Mountains and High\nTerrain South of the Yukon River.\n\n* WHEN...Until 9 PM AKST Sunday.\n\n* IMPACTS...Travel could be very difficult.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...There will be a break in the snow tonight\nthrough about 7am Sunday morning but snow will redevelop and\ncontinue through most of the afternoon and into the evening on\nSunday with lingering snow showers possible into early Monday\nmorning. There will then be another break in the snow for most of\nMonday before an additional round of snow moves in on Tuesday with\nthe potential for periods of snow persisting through Thursday\nmorning. There is high uncertainty with accumulations from Tuesday\nthrough Thursday, but an additional 6 or more inches is not out of\nthe realm of possibilities.",
                "instruction": "If you must travel, keep an extra flashlight, food, and water in\nyour vehicle in case of an emergency. Visit 511.alaska.gov or call 5\n1 1 for the latest road conditions.",
                "response": "Prepare",

                "event": "Flood Watch",
                "sender": "w-nws.webmaster@noaa.gov",
                "senderName": "NWS Los Angeles/Oxnard CA",
                "headline": "Flood Watch issued February 14 at 12:20PM PST until February 16 at 9:00PM PST by NWS Los Angeles/Oxnard CA",
                "description": "* WHAT...Flooding caused by excessive rainfall is possible. Rock and\nmud slides are possible near steep terrain. Debris flows are\npossible on burn scars.\n\n* WHERE...A portion of southwest California, including the following\nareas, most of Central and Southern Los Angeles County, all of\nVentura County, and most of Central and Southern Ventura County,\nincluding several burn scar areas.\n\n* WHEN...From Monday morning through Monday evening.\n\n* IMPACTS...Flash flooding is possible. Excessive runoff may result\nin flooding of low-lying and flood-prone locations, including\ncreeks and streams. Flooding may occur in poor drainage and urban\nareas. Low-water crossings may be flooded. Extensive street\nflooding is possible.\n\n* ADDITIONAL DETAILS...\n- A line of heavy showers and thunderstorms will sweep across\nthe region on Monday, bringing the risk for flash flooding,\nrock and mud slides, and burn-scar debris flows across the\nFlood Watch area. After the passage of the line, scattered\nheavy showers and thunderstorms will continue into Monday\nevening, prolonging the flooding risk in isolated locations.\nFlooding and burn-scar debris flows are not expected for\nareas above the snow level, of approximately 6000 feet to\n6500 feet elevation, where snow will fall instead of rain.\n- http://www.weather.gov/safety/flood",
                "instruction": "You should monitor later forecasts and be alert for possible Flood\nWarnings. Those living in areas prone to flooding should be prepared\nto take action should flooding develop. Travel through passes and\ncanyons could become dangerous, and be alert for rock slides and mud\nslides. Follow the direction of Emergency Management and Law\nEnforcement officials.",
                "response": "Prepare",
                "parameters": {
